coredata.dc:title: Development of a smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform coupled with molecular imprinting technique for in-situ determination of histamine in canned tuna
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yihan
Hua, Marti Z.
Feng, Shaolong
Lu, Xiaonan
coredata.dc:description: We reported the development of a smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform for in-situ detection and quantification of histamine in canned tuna. Molecularly imprinted polymers were synthesized via precipitation polymerization and utilized as dispersive solid phase extraction sorbent to selectively extract histamine from canned tuna. Carbon quantum dots functioning as a fluorescent probe were synthesized and introduced onto the microzones of the microfluidic paper device. This facilitated a noticeable fluorescence color change from dark red to vivid blue upon the addition of histamine. The change in fluorescence on the paper device was converted into specific RGB values using a portable UV light box combined with a smartphone. This assay achieved the limit of detection of 14.04 mg/kg with the linear range from 20 to 100 mg/kg of histamine in canned tuna. The entire molecular imprinting-microfluidic optosensing test could be completed in 45 min including sample preparation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone-based detection
μPADs
Sensing
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Histamine
Food safety

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of MIPs and adsorption tests
Molecularly imprinting dispersive solid phase extraction (MI-dSPE)
Synthesis of CQDs and fluorescence detection
Development of the smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform
Adsorption tests and characterization of MIPs
Recovery test for MI-dSPE and sample preparation
Fluorescence detection using the developed fluorescent probe
Smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform for detection of histamine in canned tuna

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the fund awarded to X.L. from Mitacs Accelerate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform for the detection of histamine in canned tuna. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) synthesized via precipitation polymerization were utilized as the dispersive solid phase extraction (dSPE) sorbent to selectively extract histamine from pre-treated canned tuna samples. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) incorporated with a red dye (ANQ) functioning as the fluorescent probe were synthesized and deposited onto the microzones of the microfluidic paper-based analytical device (µPAD). Afterwards, the collected analytical samples were introduced from the hybrid PDMS/paper sample inlet. The change in fluorescence on the paper device was converted into specific RGB values using a portable UV light box combined with a smartphone. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Development and application of the microfluidic paper-based analytical device (µPAD). (a) Fabrication of the PDMS-based holder. (b) Design and fabrication of the µPAD. (c) µPAD assembly and fluorescent probe deposition. (d) Photograph of the µPAD. (e) Photograph of the µPAD under UV light before (left) and after (right) the addition of the sample.

Characterization of the synthesized molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and non-imprinted polymers (NIPs). Static (A) and kinetic (C) binding isotherm of MIPs and NIPs for histamine in 60 % acetonitrile (n = 6). Q represents the binding capacity of the polymers. (B) and (D) are the representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images along with particle size distribution of MIPs and NIPs (insets), respectively.

Characterization of the synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (A) TEM image of CQDs. The ones circled in red dashed lines are CQDs serving as examples. Insets: high-resolution TEM image and size distribution of the CQDs. (B) FT-IR spectra of histamine, CQDs + histamine, and CQDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs. (D) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs@ANQ at various volume ratios between CQDs and ANQ. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence sensing system for histamine. Representative fluorescence spectra of (A) CQDs and (C) CQDs@ANQ after the addition of histamine at different concentrations (0–100 ppm). The correlation between fluorescence intensity ratio and histamine concentration: (B) CQDs and (D) CQDs@ANQ (n = 3). Insets: photographs of the fluorescence color change regarding the corresponding sensing system. I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of blank sample at the emission wavelength of 436 nm.

The microfluidic paper-based analytical device (µPAD). (A) Photographs of the back side of the µPADs heated at different temperatures (70, 90, and 110 °C) for various time periods (0.5, 1, 2, and 3 min). The average diameter of the hydrophilic microzones (B) and width of the connecting channels (C) are measured using Adobe Photoshop (n ≥ 4).

Determination of histamine using the smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform. (A) Photograph of fluorescence color change on the paper device upon the addition of histamine under UV light (n ≥ 3). (B) The ratio of color signal (red line) and fluorescence intensity (black line) against the concentration of histamine using µPAD as the detection platform (n = 3). (C) The ratio of color signal obtained from the developed smartphone-integrated paper-based optosensing platform against the concentration of histamine in canned tuna (n ≥ 3). The histamine level in the unspiked sample is below LOD as confirmed by HPLC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Development of a smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform coupled with molecular imprinting technique for in-situ determination of histamine in canned tuna

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Agricultural Chemistry, Faculty of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences, McGill University, Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, QC H9X 3V9, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Agricultural Chemistry
Faculty of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Agricultural Chemistry, Faculty of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences, McGill University, Sainte-Anne-de-Bellevue, QC H9X 3V9, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported the development of a smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform for in-situ detection and quantification of histamine in canned tuna. Molecularly imprinted polymers were synthesized via precipitation polymerization and utilized as dispersive solid phase extraction sorbent to selectively extract histamine from canned tuna. Carbon quantum dots functioning as a fluorescent probe were synthesized and introduced onto the microzones of the microfluidic paper device. This facilitated a noticeable fluorescence color change from dark red to vivid blue upon the addition of histamine. The change in fluorescence on the paper device was converted into specific RGB values using a portable UV light box combined with a smartphone. This assay achieved the limit of detection of 14.04 mg/kg with the linear range from 20 to 100 mg/kg of histamine in canned tuna. The entire molecular imprinting-microfluidic optosensing test could be completed in 45 min including sample preparation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine is a biogenic amine that plays a critical role in various physiological processes (e.g., inflammatory responses and allergic reactions) in the human body. Variations in the structure of receptors on cell membranes contribute to diverse histamine responses among individuals (Black, Parsons, Durant, Duncan, & Ganellin, 1972). The binding of histamine to H1 receptors may lead to a reduction in blood pressure and induce muscle contractions, while H2 receptors responding to histamine are correlated with the secretion of stomach acid (Thangam et al., 2018). Histamine is produced by decarboxylation of histidine via bacterial or tissue enzymes in certain types of fish, especially those from the Scombridae family (e.g., tuna, mackerel, and skipjack) (Peivasteh-Roudsari et al., 2020). Excessive ingestion of histamine may trigger histamine poisoning or scombroid poisoning, causing symptoms such as diarrhea, headache, nausea, and even death (Dai, Zhang, Pan, Kong, & Wang, 2014). To prevent the potential risk caused by histamine poisoning, maximum residual levels (MRLs) of histamine in food products are strictly regulated worldwide. For example, the European Community has set the MRL of histamine concentration in fish as 100 mg/kg (Zhang, Fan, Guo, Jiao, Kong, & Yu, 2022), while the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) set 50 mg/kg as the corresponding MRL (Debeer, Bell, Nolte, Arcieri, & Correa, 2021). In particular, the maximum limit of histamine level in canned tuna is normally 30 mg/kg (Gao, Grant, & Lu, 2015).

Chromatographic-based techniques are commonly applied for determination of histamine in fish products (Gagic, Jamroz, Krizkova, Milosavljevic, Kopel, & Adam, 2019), including thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Yu et al., 2018), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Simat & Dalgaard, 2011), gas chromatography (GC) (Ramezani, Hosseini, Kamankesh, Ghasemzadeh-Mohammadi, & Mohammadi, 2015), and immunological assays (Xu, Zhou, Eremin, Dias, & Zhang, 2020). These techniques can provide sensitive and accurate detection, but require highly trained personnel and cumbersome equipment. Particularly in the context of HPLC, a time-consuming and laborious pre- or post-column derivatization step is required either to change the polarity of the analyte or amplify the weak ultraviolet (UV) absorbance of aliphatic amines (Cao, Wang, Feng, Li, Xue, & Xu, 2022), extending the overall duration of the analytical process (Pérez, Bartrolí, & Fàbregas, 2013). Early stage measurement is pivotal for controlling and mitigating the impact of histamine-related foodborne illnesses. Therefore, an easy-to-use and portable detection platform that can achieve in situ detection of histamine in fish products is highly required by the fishery, aquaculture, and food industry.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials and have been regarded as the potential alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots due to their excellent photo-stability, low/non-toxicity, and low production cost (Wang et al., 2019; Zhu, Yuan, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2021). CQDs have been employed as the sensing probes for developing fluorescent platforms to detect different analytes. For instance, Shi and co-authors developed a CQDs-based fluorescent sensor integrated with peptides to specifically detect histamine in fish, and the limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 13.0 ppb with the linear range from 0.1 to 100 ppm (Shi et al., 2020). Although fluorescence-based detection can be simple and sensitive, it sometimes has unsatisfactory performance due to the interferents from food sample matrices (Zhu et al., 2021). In this case, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) can be synthesized and present a promising substitute as the sorbent for solid phase extraction (SPE) to separate and purify histamine from fish samples. MIPs are macromolecules and referred to as “plastic antibodies” for their specific recognition and binding ability to the targeted analyte(s) (Hua, Feng, Wang, & Lu, 2018), thus have been widely applied in food analysis, environmental monitoring, and biomedical diagnostics (Hu, Feng, Gao, Li-Chan, Grant, & Lu, 2015; Li, Wang, Sun, Xu, Yang, & Wang, 2016; Lu, Ming, Zhang, & Chen, 2016). Additionally, conventional fluorescence detection requires 96-well black polystyrene plates ($8–9 CAD/each) as the reaction platform and costly plate readers ($20–30 k CAD) for data collection, which is still relatively expensive and not well suited for on-site detection. The increased demand for portable and cost-effective analytical methods has contributed to promoting the development of microfluidic “lab-on-a-chip” platforms (Hua & Lu, 2020). Among them, the microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) integrates the functionalities of analytical methods on a paper-based substrate, therefore minimizing reagent volume and reducing analysis time (Ma, Nilghaz, Choi, Liu, & Lu, 2018). The natural properties of the filter paper, such as decent dry/wet strength and excellent inherent capillary effect (Hua & Lu, 2020), facilitate autonomous liquid penetration without the need for an external syringe pump that is required by polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-based microfluidic device (Raj & Chakraborty, 2020). Fluorescence detection can be conducted on μPAD as reagents and samples can be directly deposited onto the hydrophilic test zones defined by the hydrophobic barriers (e.g., wax and polystyrene) (Hua, Li, Wang, & Lu, 2018). Various fabrication approaches are available to customize different formats and patterns on μPAD, including wax printing (Chaiyo, Siangproh, Apilux, & Chailapakul, 2015), wax screen-printing (Ma et al., 2018), photolithography (Martinez, Phillips, Butte, & Whitesides, 2007), and inkjet etching (Xu, Cai, Zhong, & Zheng, 2015). To achieve rapid, simple, and low-cost fabrication, wax printing was selected in the current study for patterning and subsequent development can be easily realized using a wax printer and an oven. To the best of our knowledge, no previous work has focused on developing a µPAD-based platform to detect histamine in fish samples.

To establish a detection method that can fully achieve in-situ detection without the need for any bulky equipment, a portable UV light box coupled with a smartphone system was introduced as the signal reader (Lu, Yu, & Zhang, 2023; Wu, Zhang, Zhang, & Yu, 2024). In the current study, we developed a smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform to determine histamine in canned tuna and the schematic illustration of the experimental design is described in Fig. 1 . In brief, precipitation MIPs were synthesized and used as the sorbent to perform dispersive SPE. CQDs were synthesized and introduced onto the microzones of the μPAD as fluorescent probes. Wax printing was selected to prototype μPAD and device fabrication was conducted to optimize assay performance. The change in fluorescence color on the paper device under UV light was converted into specific RGB values using a smartphone to achieve real-time monitoring. Finally, the performance of the proposed device for sensing histamine in real food samples was evaluated.

Histamine, l-histidine, dopamine hydrochloride, tyramine, cadaverine, l-cysteine, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), dansyl chloride, NaOH, NaNO2, o-phthalaldehyde (OPA), 1-aminoanthraquinone (ANQ), and acetonitrile were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Methacrylic acid (MAA), quinine sulfate, l-Glutamine, sulfuric acid, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NH3·H2O, CuCl2, Na2S, MgSO4, K2CO3, methanol, and acetic acid were purchased from Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Bovin serum albumin (BSA) was obtained from Bioshop Canada Inc. (Burlington, ON, CA). Canned tuna samples were collected from a local grocery store in Montreal, Canada. Type 1 ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ/cm) used in the current study was prepared by the Milli-Q system (Burlington, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIPs and adsorption tests
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacities and binding equilibrium time of both MIPs and NIPs were assessed via the static and kinetic adsorption tests, respectively. For the static adsorption test, 5 mg polymers were mixed with 1 mL of histamine acetonitrile–water (60:40, v/v) solution at different concentrations (i.e., 10, 20, 40, 50, 60, and 100 ppm). The mixtures were vortexed and rotated for 2 h at room temperature, followed by centrifugation at 12,000 × g for 2 min. The amount of the unbound histamine in the supernatant was then measured by using UV–vis spectroscopy at 210 nm. The kinetic adsorption test was conducted by mixing 5 mg MIPs or NIPs with 1 mL of histamine acetonitrile–water (60:40, v/v) solution (100 ppm). The mixtures were vortexed and rotated for various time intervals (i.e., 5, 15, 30, and 60 min) at room temperature. The supernatant was collected and measured following the same procedures as for the static adsorption test. The selectivity test selecting l-histidine, dopamine, tyramine, cadaverine, and l-cysteine as the structural analogues was also performed to further evaluate the performance of the synthesized MIPs.

Canned tuna samples were immediately processed for analysis once the can was opened. Spiking of histamine and pre-treatment of canned tuna were adapted from a previous study (Gao et al., 2015). Briefly, the canned tuna meat was transferred to a sample bag and then completely homogenized using a stomacher. Then, 2 g homogenized meat was weighed and spiked with histamine stock solution to different levels ranging from 0 to 100 mg/kg, followed by vortexing for 30 s to mix thoroughly. A total of 4 mL of acetonitrile–water (60:40, v/v) was added to the mixture and vortexed for another 30 s for extraction. After centrifugation at 15,000 × g for 2 min and filtering to obtain the supernatant, the clear extract was defatted using 8 mL of hexane via another round of vortexing (30 s) and centrifugation (15,000 × g, 2 min). The hexane layer was discarded and the lower layer was stored at 4 °C for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecularly imprinting dispersive solid phase extraction (MI-dSPE)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized MIPs were employed as the sorbent for dSPE, as illustrated in Fig. 1. A total of 20 mg MIPs sorbent was added into a 2-mL microcentrifuge tube, and 1 mL of pre-treated sample extract or histamine standard solution was then introduced. MIPs were dispersed by vortexing and the suspended solution was rotated at room temperature for 5 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 2 min. After carefully removing the supernatant and vacuum drying the sorbent, 1 mL of methanol-acetic acid (90:10, v/v) was introduced into the tube for eluting the analyte (i.e., histamine) via sonication for 5 min, followed by another 2 min centrifugation at 12,000 × g. The filtered eluents (0.5 mL) were evaporated to dryness under nitrogen, redissolved in 0.5 mL of deionized water, and then directly deposited onto the sample loading zone of the developed µPAD for further analysis. MIPs were dried and recycled after MI-dSPE. The recoveries of MI-dSPE step and the entire sample preparation process were determined using HPLC-DAD at the wavelength of 254 nm. Detailed protocols of HPLC analysis can be found in Supplementary Information .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs and fluorescence detection
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize CQDs that can effectively recognize histamine, OPA was selected as the precursor as it has been used as the derivative reagent in the official AOAC method to perform post-column derivatization of detecting histamine (Bjornsdottir-Butler, Bencsath, & Benner, 2015; International, 2012). As an inert red-emitting fluorescent dye, ANQ can be integrated with the synthesized CQDs to form a color-resolvable fluorescence probe, which can contribute to a full-color emission spectrum and also overcome short-wave background interference. The synthesis procedure of the fluorescence probe was adapted from a previous study with modifications (Qin, Huang, & Wu, 2022). In general, 0.3 g OPA was dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol, and the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave lined with PTFE and sealed in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the obtained CQDs solution was adjusted to 30 mL using ethanol, followed by centrifugation at 15,000 × g for 15 min to remove the insoluble matter and filtered using 0.22-μm PTFE syringe filter to exclude large dots. The evaluation of quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CQDs is summarized in Supplementary Information . The treated CQDs solution was mixed with ANQ (1 mM in ethanol) at a volume ratio of 3:1 under sonication for 30 min to obtain the CQDs@ANQ fluorescence probe.

For fluorescence detection, both in-tube and on-paper analyses were performed. As for in-tube detection, 0.5 mL of histamine at various concentrations (i.e., 0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 ppm) were separately mixed with 0.5 mL of CQDs@ANQ. After mixing thoroughly and placing in the dark for 20 min, the fluorescence spectra (excitation = 360 nm, emission = 400–700 nm) of the mixture were recorded by using the plate reader (Tecan Spark, Männedorf, Switzerland), and the changes in fluorescence intensity (I436/I610) were compared. For on-paper detection, 20 μL of histamine at different levels (i.e., 0, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 ppm) were separately introduced into the PDMS/paper hybrid sample loading inlet of the paper device. The three reaction microzones located in the lower portion of the device were pre-loaded with CQDs@ANQ (Fig. 2 ). After complete reaction in the dark for 20 min, the center of the microzones was cut off using a 6-mm puncher. The cut-out paper sheets were put into a black 96-well plate, followed by the same procedure as described for in-tube detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform

ce:section.ce:para: Fabrication of the µPAD as the substrate for the optosensing platform can be divided into three steps: pattern design, fabrication using wax printing, and device development by heating (Fig. 2 b). The layout of the µPAD was designed using AutoCAD (Autodesk Inc., San Rafael, CA, USA), featuring four 7 mm-diameter reaction microzones. Each microzone was connected to the sample loading zone by 2 mm-wide channels, maintaining a consistent center-to-center distance of 1.1 cm between the sample loading zone and each reaction microzone. Among these, the three relatively close reaction microzones were designed to be subjected to the addition of CQDs@ANQ, facilitating multi-channel dispensing measurement. This configuration allows for triplicate measurement with a single sample addition. The reaction microzone positioned independently above was deposited with ANQ (four times diluted to match the concentration of CQDs@ANQ) serving as an internal reference as ANQ is an inert dye and insensitive to histamine. To prevent the movement of sample fluids between microzones or outside of the channels, hydrophobic barriers were created in black against a white background (Fig. 2 c).

Subsequently, the designed pattern was printed on Whatman #1 chromatography paper (GE Healthcare, Buckinghamshire, UK) using a ColorQube 8580DN printer (Xerox, Toronto, ON, Canada). The printed paper was then placed in an oven at various temperatures (i.e., 70, 90, and 110 °C) and for different durations (i.e., 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 min) to determine the optimal heating condition for device fabrication. The identified optimal condition was used to uniformly melt the wax on paper and create hydrophobic barriers on the µPAD. Additionally, a PDMS-based holder (Fig. 2 a and Fig. S2 A) was fabricated and placed exactly on the sample loading zone of the µPAD to ensure that the sample could be introduced from the center of the sample loading zone and reached all the four reaction microzones simultaneously (Fig. S2 B). The fabrication of the PDMS stamp was adapted from a previous study with modifications (Hu, Qiao, Xu, Peng, Xu, & Chen, 2017). In brief, 3 g PDMS mixture (precursor/curing agent, 10:1 w/w) was poured into a petri dish, subjected to thorough mix for 10 min, degassing, and heated at 80 °C for 30 min. Afterwards, the solid PDMS stamp was peeled off from the petri dish and shaped with a puncher to obtain the resultant holder with an outer diameter of 4 mm and an inner diameter of 2.5 mm.

To determine the required volume of both fluorescent probe pre-loaded on the microzone and sample on the µPAD, red-dyed aqueous solution at different volumes (1–50 µL) was separately added to the microzones and sample loading zone before drying the device at room temperature. The outcomes were assessed visually. The shelf life of the µPAD as well as its stability under different pH conditions were also evaluated.

A portable UV light box coupled with a smartphone was employed as the signal reader. This portable UV light box was consisted of a black plastic chamber to eliminate the interference of the external light source, a UV lamp to output 365 nm light as the excitation light source, and an observation window ∼ 25 cm away from the bottom of the box. After introducing the sample from the sample loading zone and waiting for 20 min in the dark, the µPAD was put at the center of the box. A smartphone (iPhone 13) was held directly on top of the observation window. An App “Color Analyzer” was installed and the “Analysis with camera” function was used to extract the corresponding red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values to achieve real-time detection. The standard curve relating the B/R ratio and histamine concentration could be generated to realize quantitative analysis of histamine in canned tuna.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption tests and characterization of MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
In the current study, MIPs specific to histamine were prepared via precipitation polymerization. Previous studies validated the feasibility of using MIPs as the macromolecule to separate and enrich histamine, and the polymer synthesis could be achieved using bulk polymerization (Akhoundian, Rüter, & Shinde, 2017), sol–gel polymerization (Dai et al., 2014), and precipitation polymerization (Gao et al., 2015). Among these methods, precipitation polymerization has distinct advantages, such as easy-to-operate and low-cost, and can synthesize spherical particles with uniformed size distribution (Lu, Zhu, Zhang, & Wang, 2019). To characterize the analytical performance of MIPs compared with NIPs, the adsorption capacity (Q) obtained from adsorption tests was calculated using the following equation:
Q = (
C i
-
C f
×
V W
)
, where
C f
and
C i
represent the final and initial concentration of histamine in the solution, respectively. W denotes the weight of the polymer, and V is the volume of the solution. The static adsorption isotherms indicate that QMIPs was significantly higher than QNIPs regardless of the initial concentration of histamine, while both QMIPs and QNIPs improved along with the increase of the initial concentration of histamine (Fig. 3
A). At the concentration of 100 ppm, QMIPs (11.97 mg/g) was ∼ 2 times of QNIPs (6.82 mg/g), indicating that the chemical and physical properties of histamine were successfully imprinted into MIPs to enable specific binding. The resultant recognition sites or cavities inside MIPs are complementary to the target analyte(s) in terms of size, shape, and chemical functionality (Cao, Feng, Xu, & Xue, 2019). The binding mechanism is presumably based on the hydrogen bond resulting from the reaction between the carboxylic group on MIPs and the amino group of histamine (Gao et al., 2015).

Fig. 3 C demonstrates the results of the kinetic adsorption test. Both MIPs and NIPs achieved the binding equilibrium within 5 min, confirming the feasibility of using the synthesized MIPs as macromolecules to rapidly separate histamine from canned tuna extract. In addition, higher values of QMIPs over QNIPs validated the successful imprint of histamine into MIPs. The selectivity test regarding the adsorption capacity towards several structural analogues (i.e., l-histidine, dopamine, tyramine, cadaverine, and l-cysteine) was also performed ( Fig. S3 ). Q value of histamine was significantly (P < 0.05) higher than that of all the other structural analogues, demonstrating the selective interaction between MIPs and histamine. Particularly, a negative adsorption capacity result was obtained for l-cysteine, and similar phenomena were observed in other previous studies (Gao et al., 2015; Trikka, Yoshimatsu, Ye, & Kyriakidis, 2012). This might be due to the swelling of the polymer by absorbing solvent at the given adsorption environment, resulting in increasing the concentration of l-cysteine in the solution (Gao et al., 2015). A Talos F200X G2 S Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to characterize the morphology of both MIPs and NIPs. TEM images revealed uniform spherical structure with the average particle size of 380.06 nm for MIPs and 447.47 nm for NIPs (Fig. 3 B and D). This size variation between MIPs and NIPs was consistent with that reported in another study (Lim & Holdsworth, 2018). With the average diameter < 400 nm, the synthesized MIPs might be inappropriate to be used as the sorbent to perform conventional SPE because commercial SPE frits have the average pore size ranging from 5 to 50 µm. Because dSPE could simplify SPE clean-up process and require low solvent consumption (Islas, Ibarra, Hernandez, Miranda, & Cepeda, 2017), it was selected as the sample clean-up method in the current study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery test for MI-dSPE and sample preparation

ce:section.ce:para:
CQDs using OPA as the precursor via hydrothermal method were successfully synthesized as confirmed by the optimal excitation wavelength at 360 nm and the maximum emission wavelength at 436 nm ( Fig. S4 ). Compared with the fluorescence spectrum of OPA, the apparent enhancement of the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm (Fig. S4 B) could be attributed to a higher conjugated degree of the synthesized CQDs (Barman & Patra, 2018). Both morphology and particle size of the synthesized CQDs were characterized using TEM. CQDs exhibited a consistently spherical state with the particle size of 3.06 ± 0.26 nm (Fig. 4
A). However, the edges of CQDs in the image were not distinctly clear possibly due to the background interference and scattering introduced by the carbon film (Ke, Bittencourt, & Van Tendeloo, 2015). TEM image revealed high crystallinity of the nanocrystals, forming a singular and continuous crystalline phase within individual CQDs. The lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.21 nm aligned with the (100) spacing reported for graphitic carbon (Linehan & Doyle, 2014), demonstrating the fact that the observed nanocrystals were carbon-based nanomaterials.

The chemical and structural features of the synthesized CQDs were investigated using attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transformed infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy. Distinct peaks at 1695 cm−1 and 2720 cm−1 are assigned to CO stretching and CH stretching, respectively, validating the presence of aldehyde group on the surface of the synthesized CQDs (Smith, 1998). High electronegativity of the oxygen within CO has the capability of drawing electron density from the neighboring aldehydic CH bond, thereby weakening the bond and causing a shift in the aldehydic CH stretching frequency to the wavenumber region of 2850–2700 cm−1. Meanwhile, CO present in the aromatic aldehyde is identified in the wavenumber region of 1710–1685 cm−1 due to conjugation (Fig. 4 B). As shown in the UV–vis spectrum, the synthesized CQDs exhibited three absorption peaks at 210 nm, 250 nm, and 282 nm (Fig. 4 C). The peaks at 210 nm and 250 nm correspond to the
π
-
π
* transition of both conjugated structure inside the carbon core and CC bond (Qin et al., 2022). The relatively weak absorption peak at 282 nm is attributed to the n-
π
* transition of CO bond, further confirming the existence of the aldehyde group on the surface of CQDs.

The fluorescence properties and potential applications of CQDs were then evaluated. The fluorescence QY of the synthesized CQDs was calculated to be 1.63 % ( Fig. S5 and Table S2 ). The low QY of the synthesized CQDs is probably a consequence of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) occurring from the electron donor (e.g., benzene ring) to the strong electron acceptor (e.g., aldehyde group) through its
π
-conjugated system, leading to an excited ICT state characterized by a significantly smaller band gap (Niamnont, Promchat, Siangma, Pramaulpornsatit, & Sukwattanasinitt, 2015). However, the weak fluorescent CQDs with the turn-on fluorescence feature can facilitate a noticeable color change upon the introduction of the analyte, making the detection results easily discernible by the naked eye (Niamnont et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). The stability of the synthesized CQDs and selectivity towards potential interferents were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S6 A, there was no significant difference (P > 0.05) towards the fluorescence intensity of CQDs among the pH range from 4 to 9. Fig. S6 B demonstrates the specific response between the synthesized CQDs and histamine compared to several other biogenic amines, proteins, metal cations, anions and amino acids. One possible explanation for this might be due to the steric hindrance on the surface of CQDs (Liu et al., 2019), but further study is required to investigate the working mechanism. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I436/I0) of CQDs upon the addition of histamine reached equilibrium within 20 min ( Fig. S7 ), thus enabling rapid detection. With relatively good stability and selectivity, the synthesized CQDs have the application potential to analyze complicated fish samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection using the developed fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the short-wave interference from the background (e.g., paper substrate) and achieve visual analysis, a red inert fluorescent dye ANQ was introduced into CQDs solution to form CQDs@ANQ fluorescent probe. The volume ratio of CQDs to ANQ was investigated and optimized at 3:1 as ANQ was incorporated to turn the overall visible color from bluish to purplish without covering the color signal change generated from CQDs, especially at low concentrations of histamine (Fig. 4 D). The linearity between color signal and histamine concentration at the volume ratio of 3:1 tended to be better than that of 2:1 ( Fig. S8 ).

The fluorescent sensing performance of both free CQDs and CQDs@ANQ towards histamine standard solution was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5
A-B, the fluorescence at 436 nm of free CQDs showed a gradual enhancement as the histamine concentration increased, indicating a favorable linear correlation to the concentration of histamine ranging from 1 to 100 ppm. As for the fluorescence spectra of CQDs@ANQ, two peaks at 436 nm and 610 nm were observed, corresponding to the maximum emission wavelength of CQDs and ANQ (Fig. 5 C-D). With an increase in histamine concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm steadily increased, which was similar to that of free CQDs. In comparison, emission at 610 nm remained relatively constant. The intensity ratio (I436/I610) was linearly correlated to the concentration of histamine ranging from 1 to 100 ppm. Thus, ANQ was successfully incorporated with CQDs, insensitive to histamine, contributed to altering the visible color, and enhanced the perceptibility of color changes for easy detection by the naked eye. Further study is required to have a complete understanding of the sensing mechanism between CQDs and histamine. From the FT-IR spectra of the mixed solution of CQDs and histamine (Fig. 4 B), the aforementioned signal from the aldehyde group diminished and a distinctive peak associated with CN (imine) at 1650 cm−1 emerged (Deng et al., 2020). The peak at 1328 cm−1 related to CN stretching in histamine (Bi, Tian, Zhang, Hao, & Hou, 2020) and experienced a notable reduction in intensity. Thus, the aldehyde group present on the surface of CQDs formed a Schiff base with the amino group of histamine. As reported in a previous study (Gagic et al., 2020), the enhancement of fluorescence could be facilitated by the
π
-
π
interaction between the imidazole on histamine and the benzene ring on CQDs.

Wax printing was utilized for the fabrication of µPADs as it has the advantages of printing reproducible patterns with high speed and simple operation. Whatman #1 chromatography paper sheets (7.8 × 7.8 in.) were selected because it is compatible with the Xerox printer to simplify the fabrication process. Production and performance of µPADs could be affected by different heating temperatures and times. Instead of using a hot plate to heat the wax, an oven was used to provide uniform heating to the entire paper sheet. After printing the wax patterns on the paper, the paper sheets underwent heating at various temperatures (70–90 °C) for different durations (0.5–3 min) to determine the optimal combination of temperature and time. Red-dyed aqueous solution was introduced dropwise in 2 µL volumes at four different positions on the back side of the heated paper device, and the performance of the device was verified by visually observing the state of the liquid droplets on the paper ( Fig. S9 ). For the paper heated at 70 °C and 90 °C less than 2 min, and 110 °C for 0.5 min, the printed wax could not fully penetrate the entire paper (Fig. 6
A). Either a higher temperature or a longer heating time was necessary for complete wax melting and confinement of the patterns on the paper. The diameter of the circular microzones (Fig. 6 B) and the width of the channels (Fig. 6 C) were measured to evaluate the resolution of the printed patterns. The size of the microzones heated at 90 °C for 3 min exhibited no significant difference (P > 0.05) compared to the initial diameter and showed clear wax boundaries. Thus, the optimal heating condition was determined to be 90 °C for 3 min.

The optimal volumes of fluorescent probe (i.e., CQDs@ANQ and ANQ) pre-loaded onto the detecting microzones and sample loaded from the sample loading zone were investigated, and the optimized volume was determined to be 3 µL and 20 µL, respectively ( Fig. S10 ). This was based on the fact to ensure the introduced liquid to adequately cover the designated area without reaching saturation. Besides, the stability of µPADs in solutions with different pH values was investigated to assess the impact of pH of food matrices on the hydrophilic patterned area. Acetic acid and NaCO3-NaHCO3 buffer were used to prepare the extreme acidic (pH = 1.82) and basic (pH = 9.21) solution to mimic food environment. Phosphate buffered saline was used as the neutral (pH = 7.22) solution. After immersing the developed µPADs into the prepared solutions > 12 h, excessive volume of red-dyed aqueous solution was added to evaluate the performance of the µPADs. As shown in Fig. S11 , the red dye was confined within the hydrophobic wax barrier, indicating that these wax barriers were not affected or destroyed by solutions with extreme pH. The shelf life of the µPADs stored at 4 °C in the dark was investigated over 2 weeks ( Fig. S12 ). Taken together, this µPAD is suitable for practical applications in food analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform for detection of histamine in canned tuna
ce:section.ce:para:
Histamine standard solution was introduced to evaluate the performance of this microfluidic optosensing platform. The fluorescence color changed from dark red to vivid blue upon the addition of histamine (Fig. 7
A). The color signal was converted into specific red (R), green (G), and blue (B) value using smartphone camera combined with the software. There was a linear relationship between the color ratio [(B/R)/(B/R)0] and histamine concentration at 10–100 ppm (Fig. 7 B). The corresponding LOD was 7.99 ppm as calculated using signal-to-noise (S/N) = 3. Fluorescence detection using a multiplate reader confirmed the linear relationship between the fluorescent probe signal (I436/I610) and histamine concentration. To evaluate the capability of this platform to sense histamine in food samples, canned tuna spiked with concentrations of 20–100 mg/kg were prepared and analyzed. A linear regression was observed between the signal intensity and spiking concentration (Fig. 7 C). It achieved an LOD of at 14.04 mg/kg within the linear range of 20–100 mg/kg of histamine, which met the requirement of MRL set in both Europe and North America. This assay does not include time-consuming and labor-intensive sample preparation as that required for HPLC pre-column derivatization (Altieri, Semeraro, Scalise, Calderari, & Stacchini, 2016). Taken together, the entire test including sample preparation and detection could be completed in 45 min.

In the current study, a low-cost, equipment-free, and user-friendly smartphone-integrated microfluidic paper-based optosensing platform coupled with molecular imprinting technique was developed to determine histamine in canned tuna. As the macromolecule, the synthesized MIPs could effectively extract histamine in canned tuna via MI-dSPE with the recovery of 91.9–94.41 %. CQDs@ANQ functioning as the fluorescent probe was synthesized and introduced onto the microzones of the µPADs. The change in fluorescence on the paper device was converted into specific RGB values using a portable UV light box combined with a smartphone. This proposed assay could achieve an LOD of 14.04 mg/kg with the linear range of 20–100 mg/kg of histamine in canned tuna, satisfying the MRL regulation set in both Europe and North America. The entire test could be completed in 45 min including sample preparation. Future study is suggested to evaluate the analytical performance of this assay for determination of histamine in other agri-food products, such as alcohol and other fermented beverages, dried fruits, and processed meats.

Yihan He: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Marti Z. Hua: Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shaolong Feng: Methodology, Data curation. Xiaonan Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the fund awarded to X.L. from Mitacs Accelerate.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139446.
